findings.suggestion:
Sanitize before rendering: `import DOMPurify from 'dompurify'` then `
` with an explicit tag/attribute allowlist. If HTML is not actually required, render `
{body}
` so React escapes it. Add a rendering test that mounts Comment with `body=''` and asserts the handler is stripped; sanitize on the server side as well since the input is untrusted.
findings.summary:
source.tsx:12 `return
;` injects the `body` prop — documented at line 5 as 'user-supplied comment markdown/html' — directly into the DOM as raw HTML. No sanitization or allowlisting occurs anywhere in the component (lines 8-13). An attacker-authored comment containing event-handler or javascript: payloads (e.g. ``, `
findings.title: Reflected/stored XSS: user-supplied `body` rendered via dangerouslySetInnerHTML without sanitization

findings.suggestion: Sanitize before rendering, e.g. `DOMPurify.sanitize(body)`, or render `{body}` as escaped text / use a sanitizing markdown renderer if HTML is not required. Never pass untrusted input to dangerouslySetInnerHTML unsanitized.
findings.summary: source.tsx:12 renders `body` — typed and documented as "user-supplied comment markdown/html" (source.tsx:5) — directly via `dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: body }}` with no sanitization. This is a confirmed XSS sink: a comment body like `` executes in every viewer's session on load. React's dangerouslySetInnerHTML bypasses JSX escaping, so nothing mitigates this in the path from prop to sink.
findings.title: Unsanitized user content in dangerouslySetInnerHTML — stored/reflected XSS

findings.suggestion:
Sanitize before rendering — e.g. `DOMPurify.sanitize(body)` with a strict config, or render `body` as plain text (`
{body}
`) if HTML is not required. Do NOT fix by only stripping `
findings.summary: source.tsx:12 renders `dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: body }}` where `body` is documented as user-supplied content (source.tsx:5). This bypasses React's JSX escaping and injects attacker markup into the DOM. The component relies on an unstated, unenforced invariant that `body` is safe HTML — no sanitizer, branded type, or allowlist exists. Any viewer rendering a hostile comment is exposed to script execution (session/cookie theft, account takeover). Currently 0 callers, so no live exposure yet, but the vulnerability is real the moment it is wired up.
findings.title: XSS: user-supplied `body` rendered via dangerouslySetInnerHTML without sanitization

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

A newly added React `Comment` component renders user-supplied `body` directly through `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` with no sanitization — a confirmed XSS. The code documents its own invariant contradiction (prop is 'user-supplied' at line 5, yet treated as trusted HTML at line 12) and the guiding comment at line 10 is technically inaccurate about `<script>` execution, which could misdirect a naive fix. No callers yet (blast radius 0), so no live exposure, but the pattern is high severity once wired to real data. No fresh browser/verification artifact was attached; static analysis alone confirms the flaw here.

New Comment component introduces a critical XSS vulnerability: user-supplied `body` flows unsanitized into `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` (source.tsx:12). Confirmed by static analysis — the prop is explicitly documented as untrusted (line 5) and reaches the raw-HTML sink with no escaping or sanitization. Exploitable via event-handler payloads (onerror/onload), impacting every user who views an attacker's comment. Symbol has 0 callers (newly added), so it is not yet live, but must be fixed before wiring in. UI verification gates remain blocked (no browser/Playwright artifact), but the vulnerability is provable without runtime evidence.

Single new 13-line React component with a critical, self-described XSS sink: user-supplied `body` flows into dangerouslySetInnerHTML with no sanitization (source.tsx:12). The agent left an accurate `// BUG:` comment but shipped the vulnerable code — a fake-green handoff. No sanitizer dependency, no test, and no verification artifact accompany the change; both evidence gates (fresh verification, UI/render proof) remain legitimately blocked. This must be treated as an unremediated critical vulnerability, not a known-accepted note.
